observations.recommendation: Require immediate settlement of the audit disallowances having been issued with COE to avoid further legal actions as prescribed by the aforementioned governing rules and regulations of the Circular. Likewise, strict compliance with laws, rules and regulations on the disbursements of funds to avoid issuances of audit suspensions, disallowances and charges in the future.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to take up the necessary adjusting journal entries to reclassify the recording of the 10 percent retention monies totaling ₱3,119,688.86 to the proper liability accounts to ensure fair presentation of the balances of the accounts as presented in the year-end financial statements.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant take up the necessary adjusting journal entries in the books of accounts to reflect the actual insurance expenses incurred and recognize the unexpired balance under Prepaid Insurance account for CY 2024.

Henceforth, faithfully comply with the provisions of IPSAS regarding the application of accrual basis of accounting and strictly observe the proper use of accounts as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.

We recommended that Management:
Submit legal bases or any exemption to the law and/or concurrence of COMELEC for further consideration in audit for the appointment of the Sectoral Representative for Agriculture; and
Explain why the payments of salaries, RATA, communication allowances, bonuses and other emoluments should not be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee shall submit justification on the mode of procurement made through public bidding instead of determining first the applicability of negotiated procurement-community participation.
Further, to improve the effectiveness of the program, we recommended that Management strictly adhere to the provisions prescribed under Item B(4), Section VI of DSWD Memorandum Circular No. 26, series of 2024, as amended, in relation to GPPB Resolution No. 18-2021 dated October 22, 2021.

We recommended that the LGU:
Ensure that all procurement be meticulously and judiciously planned in order to avoid idle/unused equipment and to warrant the economy and efficiency of spending government funds;
Ensure that idle and unused medical equipment are stored properly in a good/suitable condition to prevent them from further deterioration or obsolescence for non-use; and
Facilitate the immediate installation and provision of the necessary licensing permit of medical equipment upon completion of the rehabilitation of the RHU to avoid delay in the delivery of additional health service to its constituents.

We, therefore, recommended that the Municipal General Services Officer (MGSO), being jointly and solidarily liable with the persons cleared and primarily accountable either pay the residual value of the fully depreciated properties involved or request for relief of accountability with complete documentary requirements as provided under COA Memorandum No. 1992-751 dated February 24, 1992.
Moving forward, the MGSO must strictly exercise the diligence of a good father of a family in order to strengthen the internal control of the agency in safeguarding its assets, to prevent the incurrence of loss or misuse of government funds and property in pursuance to Sections 104 and 105 of Presidential Decree (P.D.) No. 1445.

We therefore recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal General Services Officer to a.) prepare the required Registry of Semi-Expendable Property Issued (RegSPI) and Report Semi-Expendable Property Issued (RSPI) and b.) observe the proper maintenance of Inventory Custodian Slips (ICS) in conformity with COA Circular No. 2022-004 dated May 31, 2022.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Management:
Prioritize the disposal proceedings and derecognize unserviceable properties from the accounting books and property records upon disposal thereof based on the approved Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP); and
Pursue the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 to finally establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability. Specifically, observe the disposition procedures for non-existing/missing PPEs enumerated in Paragraph 7 of the same circular.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal General Services Officer (MGSO) present and submit the Official Receipts and Certificates of Registration of the remaining motor vehicles to the Audit Team to prove their ownership and facilitate the process of registration and/or insurance, if applicable.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), through its secretariat, submit all contracts, together with all the supporting documents forming part thereof, within five days from its perfection to the Auditor to enable the latter to conduct auditorial review, before these contracts are forwarded to COA Regional Office for technical evaluation.
In addition, we also recommended that Management assess the workload of the personnel in-charge of the submission of contract documents, with the view of ensuring greater efficiency in the delivery of public services. This assessment should also help determine if there is a need for additional personnel to support this function.

We, therefore, recommended that the GFPS:
Conduct a gender analysis of their programs and projects through the administration of the HGDG tool to be able to incorporate the PPAs that address gender issues in the GPB as well as mainstreaming in the agency operations so that the formulation, implementation, evaluation, and accounting of results of the LGU’s annual GPB and GAD AR will be in order;
Build capabilities through trainings to deepen their knowledge and build their competencies on GAD-related laws and international commitments, gender mainstreaming, gender analysis, gender-responsive planning and budgeting, and GAD-related tools among others; and
Submit the DILG-reviewed GPB and GAD AR within the period required by Part V of COA Circular No. 2014-001 dated March 18, 2014.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the LDRRMO to continuously monitor the implementation of PPAs to facilitate the timely completion thereof;
Ensure that PPAs funded out of the DRRM funds are well-planned and meticulously reviewed by the LDRRMC; and
Instruct the LDRRMO to prepare the LDRRMFIP as prescribed in COA Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 for the current year. Additionally, in a separate section, includes the list of project and activities to be funded from the unexpended LDRRMF in previous years under the Special Trust Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant continue his effort in complying within the timely preparation of Bank Reconciliation Statements for fair presentation of Cash in Bank account of the Municipality.

Conduct immediate repair damages noted on the project from the force majeure to prevent its further damage and deterioration.
The Municipal Engineer inform the contractor to replace the damaged Phenolic Boards as noted in the technical inspection report.

System (RPCLRN) supported with Local Road Network Maps

dated November 25, 2015
b. Municipal General Services Officer

2. Keep a complete Local Road Network Property Cards for all roads
and its components and submit the same to the Audit Team (Annex
E format) per Item VII b.2 of COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated
November 25, 2015.
c. Municipal Accountant
1. Submit the required copies of PPE Local Road Network Ledger
Cards (Annex D format) per Item VII a.1 of COA Circular No.

2. Update the yearly required disclosures as needed;

4. Prepare a lapsing schedule regularly for the computation of the

d. Municipal Engineer

Services Officer with the complete description and cost segregation
of road components for road projects as per Item VII.c.1 of COA

and if found to have no legitimate claim against the Municipality, cause the
reversion of the same to the government equity.

Trust Fund Books

Accountant to recognize the liability of the Municipality to DA-RFO 02
totaling P2,148,000.00. Also, recognize the corresponding receivable accounts
from the recipients for fair representation of the Municipality’s financial
statements. In case of doubt, management should make representations with
DA-RFO 02 immediately to clarify the status of their unsettled account.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant make full-disclosures in the
financial statements on the impact and overall risk of COVID-19 in LGUs
operations to be faithfully represented and be more reliable.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer carefully prepare and
evaluate the Program of Works of a project with reference to the Detailed

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management through the Accounting Office, reclassify the Land (Market Lot) and the Market Asset accounts to the Investment Property, Land and the Investment Property, Building accounts to conform with IPSAS 16-Investment Property, for fair presentation of the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant present with full disclosure in the notes to financial statements the amortization of the computer software of the LGU for fair presentation and reliability of information.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor in coordination with the Sangguniang Bayan, adopt and implement guidelines taking into consideration the following:
Review the amounts of cell card load allowance in relation to the available postpaid lines which are in reasonable amount per month for heads of offices/elected officials taking into account the prevailing rates or available promos offered by various telecommunication service providers. At any rate, any excess on the limit set, shall be for the personal account of the user.
Utilize alternatives modes of communication such as internet for long-distance communication or consider the features of communication network of its free long distance calls within the region that provides cost savings measures.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the concerned offices to observe the due dates of submission of the required financial reports and documents to the Audit Team, particularly on the reports pertaining to COVID-19 response in accordance with Section 347, Chapter 4, Title V, Book II of Republic Act 7160 and Section 7.2.1 paragraph (a) of COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009.

We recommended that the LGU continue being compliant with the reporting requirements under Paragraph 4 of LBC No. 125 dated April 7, 20120 as to funds received from Bayanihan Grant. However, we also require the Management to submit written justifications indicating the reason behind the non-submission of written notices to the House of Representatives, Senate of the Philippines, House Committee on Appropriations, and Senate Committee on Finance.

We recommended that the LGU submit a one-time report on Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances, instead of monthly reports to be supported by the Acknowledgement receipts of the donations in-kind, proof of receipt by and distribution to beneficiaries, and the inventory of remaining undistributed items for verification and validation of proper valuation of balances. Copy of the report should be submitted to the National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council and COA within ten (10) days after the end of each quarter.

We recommended that the LGU thru the Municipal Accountant make a full disclosure in the Notes to FS in the succeeding report of all donations, subsidies and transfers received and utilized related to COVID-19 outbreak and other related/similar fortuitous events in compliance with COA Circular No. 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014 and COA Circular No. 2020-009 dated April 21, 2020. Also, as part of LGU’s management action, coordinate with the National Food Authority, Isabela Branch Office, Santiago City for the noted discrepancies in the confirmation of receipt of donated rice from their end.

We recommended that Management enjoin the Bids and Awards Committee, thru the TWG, of the LGU to judiciously conduct the post evaluation of bids to ensure strict compliance on the sufficiency of the Contractor’s manpower and equipment on all projects to be awarded and not on an individual project only especially in cases where only one bidder bid in several projects which have the same or overlapping implementation period. Strict adherence to the provisions of RA 9184 is advised.
Further, we recommended that management invite more qualified bidders to participate in the public bidding to undertake a more competitive procurement activity which will result in a more acceptable implementation of infrastructure projects.

We recommended that Management continue to ensure that copies of contracts be furnished to the Auditor within five (5) working days from its execution and shall be supported with complete documents as required in COA Circular 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.

We recommended that management require all officials and employees responsible in the preparation of driver’s trip tickets to record the odometer readings before and after the trip for all vehicles of the Municipality to ascertain the actual distance travelled for each motor vehicle. Implement strictly the provisions of the Manual on Audit for Fuel Consumption of Government Motor Vehicles prescribed in COA Cir. No. 77-61 dated September 26, 1977 for effective control of gasoline, oil and lubricants expenditures.
Likewise, exercise control over the use of government vehicles through properly accomplished Driver’s Trip Tickets. A summary of which shall be made at the end of the month in a Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption and Monthly Report of Official Travels for audit verification purposes to determine the reasonableness of fuel consumed during the period.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Treasurer stamp “PAID” all disbursement vouchers along with all accompanying supporting documents after payment to avoid its possible re-use, thus safeguarding cash from loss thru double payments.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Treasurer direct the Accountable Officer in-charge in the preparation of checks to be cautious in entering data to avoid the frequent error and waste of government resources. Moreover, all cancelled checks be attached to the RCI and a copy of which be submitted to the auditor’s office for review and verification.

We recommended that Management ensure (a) the documents required by the Technical Inspector Specialist be submitted thru the Audit Team for evaluation purposes; (b) the contractor for the catch-up program in order to fast track the completion of the project to avoid rescindment of contract as stipulated in Section 68 of RA 9184; (c) the conduct of inventory of work accomplished by the contractor and impose liquidated damages at least equal to one-tenth of one percent (0.1%) of the cost of the unperformed portion for every day of delay; and (d) to intensify monitoring of project in order to avoid recurrence of delay to other projects.

We recommended that Management require (a) the Municipal Engineer to submit the documents required by the Technical Inspector Specialist thru the Audit Team for evaluation purposes as well as letter-explanation on the issued “Certification” of project completion ahead of the date of the Statement of Work Accomplished; (b) the contractor for the catch-up program in order to fast track the completion of projects to avoid rescindment of contract as stipulated in Section 68 of RA 9184; (c) the conduct of inventory of work accomplished by the contractor and impose liquidated damages at least equal to one-tenth of one percent (0.1%) of the cost of the unperformed portion for every day of delay; and (d) to intensify monitoring of project in order to avoid recurrence of delay to other projects.

We recommended that Management (a) submit a letter-justification from the Municipal Engineer on the noted deficiencies and to comply with the needed documents in order to facilitate the proper costing of the project; (b) require the BAC on the complete posting requirements such as “quantity” of the work items to be bid out in the PhilGEPS.

We recommended to Management the following:
Implement the development projects programmed during the year as embodied in the Annual Investment/Development Plan and ensure optimum utilization of the 20% Development Fund in compliance with the provisions of DBM, DOF and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1 dated November 4, 2020 for the benefit of the constituents of the Municipality;
Implement immediately all the projects under the continuing appropriations; and
At the end of each year, review all unimplemented projects and verify whether they can be implemented in the succeeding year. All savings from completed projects and all projects which are no longer feasible and cannot be implemented should be considered for immediate realignment, subject to budgetary rules on reversion to the unappropriated surplus of the 20% Development Fund for re-appropriation to other development projects.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive thru the Solid Waste Management Board make representation to the PGI for the matters relative to the pilot sanitary landfill as soon as the clustered municipalities will convene to conform with the requirements of Sections 40 and 41 of RA 9003 in order to achieve an effective solid waste disposal system for the Municipality without prejudice to the administrative sanction provided in Section 50 of R.A. No. 9003. Likewise, instruct the MENRO-Designate to furnish the audit team a copy of MOA and other documents from the PGI relative to the establishment of clustered sanitary landfill.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Office of the Municipal Treasurer be provided with steel protection in the glass window counters and put up a door enclosure to safeguard the overall office.  Likewise, steel cabinets be furnished to safely keep the cashbooks and accountable forms.

We recommended that Management undertake proper disposal of identified unserviceable/obsolete PPEs and the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting entry after disposal to record the derecognition of properties.

We recommended that Management, thru the Municipal Accountant, submit certified copies of disbursement vouchers and supporting documents showing that corresponding liquidated damages were already deducted from the contractors’ claims on the above projects, if any.  Further, the Municipal Engineer in coordination with the MPDC to (a) ensure that the contractors engage catch-up program in order to fast track the completion of the projects with noted delays to avoid rescindment of contract as stipulated in Section 68 of RA 9184; (b) conduct an inventory of work accomplished by the contractor and impose liquidated damages of at least equal to one-tenth of one percent (0.1%) of the cost of the unperformed portion for every day of delay; and (c) intensify monitoring of project in order to avoid recurrence of delay to other projects by submission of Statement of Time Elapsed and Work Accomplished for all infrastructure projects.

We recommended that Management ascertain valid charges against Other Professional Services and set aside the grant of incentives in the form of honoraria/subsidy to national government employees and others until the Municipality has fully implemented the Modified Salary Standardization Schedule and other conditions prescribed in DBM Compensation Policy Guidelines 98-1.

We recommended that Management refrain from paying registration fees supported by receipts issued directly by the hotel venue and require official receipts from the sponsoring agency or group for complete documentation of government expenditures.  On the other hand, the disbursements of the barangays may be held in trust and accounted for by the Municipality and any payments thereof be done by the latter with transparency and accountability in absence of official receipt to be issued by the sponsoring agency.
We recommended that the Management desist from hiring a Legal Officer without strictly complying the provisions Section 481 of Republic Act (RA) No. 7160 and COA Circular No. 98-002.

We recommended that the Management fast track the prompt implementation of all projects/programs funded out of the financial assistance given by the Provincial Government of Isabela in order to give the constituents early benefits that could be derived therefrom otherwise return the fund to the source agency consistent with Section 4.9 and 6.7 of COA Circular No. 94-013.

We recommended that the Management intensify its efforts to have the 10-Year Ecological Solid Waste Management Plan for 2018-2027 approved by the National Solid Waste Management Commission to achieve its vision of having an economically stable, healthy, safe, adaptive, resilient God loving and law-abiding citizenry living in a peaceful, orderly and ecologically sound environment with a responsive and dynamic leadership.
We also recommended that Management comply all applicable rules and regulations of the Ecological Solid Waste Management Act of 2000 to ensure protection of public health and environment by closing the open dump site, and operate a sanitary landfill as a final disposal site for solid and eventually, residual wastes of the municipality.

We recommended that Management submit immediately to the Audit Team the complete projects documents of the remaining seventeen (17) projects as stated in the schedules of this Audit Observation Memorandum.  Likewise, ensure thereafter the submission of all contracts, purchase orders and the like together with the required supporting documents to facilitate auditorial review, evaluation and inspection in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-001.

We recommended that Management require immediate settlement of the audit disallowances having been issued with COE to avoid further legal actions as prescribed by the aforementioned governing rules and regulations of the Circular. Likewise, strict compliance with laws, rules and regulations on the disbursements of funds to avoid issuances of audit suspensions, disallowances and charges in the future.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant (a) continue being compliant with the revenue regulations on the withholding of taxes from compensation of employees and creditable taxes for payments to suppliers and contractors and the remittance thereof within the prescribed period; (b) continue with the efforts to resolve and reconcile the carry-over discrepancy on the Due to BIR account balance and effect immediately the remittance of the overdue balance.
We recommended that the Petty Cash Fund Custodian record, maintain and certify her PCF cashbook/register as required under COA Circular No. 2003-006 dated December 2, 2003.

observations.managementAction: The Management already made the necessary justifications for the non-inclusion of the said amount in the Cash in Bank balance as of December 31, 2018.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record the results of confirmed balances from the creditors in the LGU books to reflect the outstanding loan balances as at year-end. Likewise, reconcile with loan creditors from time to time to update loan balances and to avoid under/overstatement of balances, affecting the fair presentation thereat.

Likewise, we recommended that Certificate of Acceptance be issued one (1) year after the completion of the project in order to hold the contractor for the repair of defects in consonance with Section 62.2.2 of 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184.

Likewise, we recommended that the Certificate of Acceptance be issued one (1) year after the completion of the project in order to hold the contractor for any repair of defects in consonance with Section 62.2.2 of 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184.
observations.managementAction: The repairs of the noted deficiencies in the inspected projects have been made. Likewise, all certificate of acceptance on all other projects have been issued only one (1) year after the completion of the projects.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly adhere to the accounting and reporting of LDRRMF especially the Paragraph 5.1.10, Section 5.0, of COA Circular No. 2012-002. Likewise, take up the necessary adjustments in the General and Trust Fund books to reflect the correct balance of the Trust Liabilities-DRRMF account.

Lastly, exact locations of succeeding projects were already indicated in each project.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management  fully observe the provisions of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017 on the use of 20% Development Fund, in order that proper utilization and implementation of the developmental projects be carried out effectively and efficiently. Ensure that the utilization of the 20% Development Fund be limited to programs, projects and activities that will contribute to the attainment of socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes and shall partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures and yield greater benefit to the people.

We also recommended that the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator  follow the guidelines prescribed in DILG-DBM JC No. 2017-1 to ensure coordination of activities and proper implementation and utilization of 20% Development Fund by aligning expenditures with identified activities in the approved Annual Investment Plan.

Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant refrain from charging repairs and maintenance expenses to 20% Development Fund and to charge the same instead to MOOE and/or Capital Outlay under the General Fund Proper.
observations.managementAction: The PPAs under the 2019 Annual Budget still include items which are not allowed under the JMC but the same will no longer be implemented, instead be reprogrammed to other projects allowed therein. The projects disallowed by the SP in the 2018 Annual Budget has been submitted to the SB for reprogramming.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management optimally implement the LDRRMF programs and see to it that all expenses incurred are in accordance with the approved budget/investment program to strengthen the capacity of the LGU to build the disaster resilience of communities and enhance disaster preparedness and response capabilities at all levels.  Likewise, programs/projects/activities be specifically indicated in the LDRRMFIP and Monthly Report of Utilization of LDRRMF under a separate caption together with which and how it is to be funded if taken from the current year’s appropriation, continuing, special trust fund or from outside sources.
observations.managementAction: The proposed reprogramming of the unutilized fund was not approved immediately by the LDRRM Council hence, it is only submitted recently in the SB for their approval.

Review of the contracts of all COS and job order employees has been made as a result, some of their contracts has been amended so that their job title and functions will fit their actual duties and functions. Likewise, payment of their salaries has been properly supported with the required documentation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant implement the monthly net take home pay for each employee pursuant to Section 48 of General Provisions of the General Appropriations Act (GAA) for FY 2018 also known as Republic Act No. 10964 and comply for the succeeding GAA issuances to maintain the required net take home pay as it changes from time to time.

Likewise, we recommended that the Authorized Approving Officer (AAO) for various loans coordinate with the Municipal Accountant before endorsing loans to consider the loan applicant’s Certificate of Net Pay duly certified by the Municipal Accountant in order to avoid instances wherein pay slips are modified in order to make his/her net pay appear to have increased to accommodate additional loan deductions in cases of applying of a specific loan.
observations.managementAction: Although a memorandum has been issued restricting all officials and employees from availing various loans if such will impair or affect their NTHP, there are still employees whose NTHP are below P5,000.00 and such will remain to be the same until such time that their loans will be fully paid because we are obliged to deduct what is billed to them.
observations.reason: Since the condition still exists, said observation is to be implemented fully upon loans of employees were already been paid.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer provide the audit team with legal bases of charging the said honoraria under the Personal Services and not an item in the Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses or Capital Outlay in LGU budgets, as the case may be, as required by Section 4.1.2 of the Local Budget Circular No. 98 dated October 14, 2011 as supported by COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.

Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant support the transactions under Check payments to MLGOOs such as DILG Office Order/s showing designations of said DILG personnel, for our review, verification and appropriate action.
observations.managementAction: As of February 11, 2019 when the AOM was received, no honorarium for MLGOO was charged under PS although there is an appropriation in the 2019 Annual Budget for such. Likewise, the Office Order and the Certificate of Assumption of the two MLGOOs mentioned has been submitted to the Audit Team. As of to date, the proposed realignment to fund the Other Professional Services under the MOOE is not yet approved hence, payment of the honoraria of the MLGOO from February to May 2019 is deferred.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer ensure that the disbursement vouchers pertaining to grant of cash advances be based on the net amount of the payroll and not on the list of payments/liquidation of payroll and the same shall be used as supporting documents of the disbursement vouchers before submission to the Audit Team for post-audit.
observations.managementAction: An additional copy of the approved payrolls has been attached to the cash advance voucher to support such.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

Furnish the Audit team with any existing written policy on the granting of financial assistance that would systematically determine the extent of aid to be given to individuals in need.

Coordinate with the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Office to assess request for all assistance by way of Social Case Study Reports so that only the deserving indigents can be assisted and served. Beneficiaries be properly screened giving priority to the less privileged constituents.

Instruct the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Office to assist the applicants and require a complete submission of supporting documents for the assistance being requested as a basis of assessment and recommendation to make sure that only the deserving ones are provided with the necessary assistance.

In absence of any policy, create guidelines on the grant of donation/financial assistance containing the following:

Type of financial assistance (medical, burial etc.)
Approval limits for specific cases.
Criteria in granting the donation/financial assistance and the documentary requirements for each particular case.
Guidelines for the selection and retention of student-individual entitled for educational assistance.

Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure all disbursements for financial assistance be supported with appropriate and complete documentation pursuant to Section 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445.
observations.managementAction: Although the required SDs has been attached to the DVs and such is continuously monitored, the guidelines as provided in the AO issued by the DSWD cannot strictly implemented in the LGU because various factors which were discussed in our management reply.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management refrain the practice of paying for meals and snacks of officials and employees during ordinary staff meeting and Sangguniang Bayan (SB) sessions that are regular or normal activities in the LGU’s operations or part of the duties of the concerned officials/employees in order to prevent the incurrence of irregular and unnecessary expenditures and to ensure that government resources are used equitably and wisely.

Further, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant continue in checking the propriety and validity of the claims on meals and snacks unless these are duly supported with proper and complete documentation.
observations.managementAction: No payment of meals and snacks made beginning from the date of receipt of the AOM is such was served during activities which are regular or normal in the operation of the LGU or part of the duties of the concerned officials and employees. Also, necessary supporting documents has been attached to vouchers for payment of meals and snacks.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer observe the release of the barangay share from RPT collections on a quarterly basis, in order for the barangay to utilize the fund for its purpose.  Likewise, ensure that the payments are duly supported with complete documentation.
observations.managementAction: Although the transfers/release of the barangay share from RPT were made on a quarterly basis, the same was not made  within five (5) days after the end of each quarter as required by RA 7160 because factors previously mentioned in the management reply.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management exercise prudence and economy in the utilization of government funds by limiting the practice of undertaking travel as such that could have been used to finance more viable projects that would contribute to the improvement of general public. Likewise, require the concerned officials to render justification on the incurrence of said expenses. Moreover, adhere to the rules and regulation cited above to adopt austerity measures in Government.
observations.managementAction: The SB Members already submitted the required justification. As of the date of the receipt of the AOM, customary mode of transportation was already availed whenever attending to official business.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management ensure that copies of contracts be furnished to the Auditor within five (5) working days from its execution and shall be supported with complete documents as required in COA Circular 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.  Likewise, the Municipal Engineer monitor and effect the inclusion of all awarded projects in his quarterly report of government projects.

Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Engineer coordinate with the Municipal Accountant and the Bids Chairman to furnish the Audit Team with the lacking documents specified in the Annex B and regularly observe completeness of contract documents submitted.
observations.managementAction: Perfected contracts including its supporting documents were still submitted late due to factors beyond control as discussed in the management reply.

No. 9003. Likewise, instruct the MENRO-Designate to furnish the audit team a copy of MOA and other documents from the PGI relative to the establishment of clustered sanitary landfill.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Petty Cash Fund Custodian record, maintain and certify her PCF cashbook/register as required under COA Circular No. 2003-006 dated December 2, 2003.

We recommended that Management justify the non-inclusion of the Fiesta Fund bank account in the LGU books and the explanation of its closure on January 2019 in order to give merit on the reported cash in bank balances as of year-end totaling to P48,973,875.64.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record the results of confirmed balances from the creditors in the LGU books to reflect the outstanding loan balances as at year-end. Likewise, reconcile with loan creditors from time to time to update loan balances and to avoid under/overstatement of balances, affecting the fair presentation thereat.

We recommended that Management require the Contractor to undertake repair immediately on the deficiencies noted to conform with Section 62.2.2.1 and Item 5.3 (e) of Annex E of 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No. 9184, otherwise the amount of P41,953.95 be deducted from the unpaid claims of the contractor.

Likewise, we recommended that Certificate of Acceptance be issued one (1) year after the completion of the project in order to hold the contractor for the repair of defects in consonance with Section 62.2.2 of 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that Management require the Contractor to undertake completely the deficiency noted, otherwise the amount of P90,882.00 will be disallowed in audit.

Likewise, we recommended that the Certificate of Acceptance be issued one (1) year after the completion of the project in order to hold the contractor for any repair of defects in consonance with Section 62.2.2 of 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that all submitted documents are duly signed by the concerned LGU Officials  and all material information needed be provided.

Likewise, we recommend that issuance of Certificate of Acceptance should be one (1) year after the completion of the project in order to hold the contractor for any repair of defects.

We recommended that the Management  fully observe the provisions of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017 on the use of 20% Development Fund, in order that proper utilization and implementation of the developmental projects be carried out effectively and efficiently. Ensure that the utilization of the 20% Development Fund be limited to programs, projects and activities that will contribute to the attainment of socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes and shall partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures and yield greater benefit to the people.
We also recommended that the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator  follow the guidelines prescribed in DILG-DBM JC No. 2017-1 to ensure coordination of activities and proper implementation and utilization of 20% Development Fund by aligning expenditures with identified activities in the approved Annual Investment Plan.
Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant refrain from charging repairs and maintenance expenses to 20% Development Fund and to charge the same instead to MOOE and/or Capital Outlay under the General Fund Proper.

We recommended that Management optimally implement the LDRRMF programs and see to it that all expenses incurred are in accordance with the approved budget/investment program to strengthen the capacity of the LGU to build the disaster resilience of communities and enhance disaster preparedness and response capabilities at all levels.  Likewise, programs/projects/activities be specifically indicated in the LDRRMFIP and Monthly Report of Utilization of LDRRMF under a separate caption together with which and how it is to be funded if taken from the current year’s appropriation, continuing, special trust fund or from outside sources.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
assess if there is really a need for consultants with the following considerations (i) engage only experts in their respective fields by considering the desired expertise, which is not available among the regular staff, in hiring consultant;  ; (ii) observe rules and regulations on the hiring of consultants as provided by IRR of RA No. 9184 and COA Circular 2012-003 dated October 29, 2012;
specify the consultants’ duties/functions in the contracts as bases in the enforcement of their deliverables with the end in view to maximizing the worth of hiring said consultants and minimizing monetary losses to the Municipality;
If warranted, amend the contracts of the consultants to properly match that of the needs of the LCE or any officials needing assistance thereof to eliminate the nature of hiring said consultants; and
ensure that payments for consultancy services are supported with adequate documentation by the Municipal Accountant before processing and payment of the claims for wages/salaries of the consultants to avoid Notice of Suspension/Disallowance in audit; and
evaluate and utilize the skills and talents of their regular employees to cut cost and to boost their morale; Likewise, utilize the services of the casual employees in discharging regular/ordinary services to compensate and maximize  the costs of their employment.
We likewise recommended that the Human Resource Management Officer clearly facilitate and define the works or activities which could only be accomplished through hiring of consultants, incorporate them in the contracts and made them the bases to justify the need for consultancy services.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant implement the monthly net take home pay for each employee pursuant to Section 48 of General Provisions of the General Appropriations Act (GAA) for FY 2018 also known as Republic Act No. 10964 and comply for the succeeding GAA issuances to maintain the required net take home pay as it changes from time to time.

Likewise, we recommended that the Authorized Approving Officer (AAO) for various loans coordinate with the Municipal Accountant before endorsing loans to consider the loan applicant’s Certificate of Net Pay duly certified by the Municipal Accountant in order to avoid instances wherein pay slips are modified in order to make his/her net pay appear to have increased to accommodate additional loan deductions in cases of applying of a specific loan.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer provide the audit team with legal bases of charging the said honoraria under the Personal Services and not an item in the Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses or Capital Outlay in LGU budgets, as the case may be, as required by Section 4.1.2 of the Local Budget Circular No. 98 dated October 14, 2011 as supported by COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.

Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant support the transactions under Check payments to MLGOOs such as DILG Office Order/s showing designations of said DILG personnel, for our review, verification and appropriate action.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer ensure that the disbursement vouchers pertaining to grant of cash advances be based on the net amount of the payroll and not on the list of payments/liquidation of payroll and the same shall be used as supporting documents of the disbursement vouchers before submission to the Audit Team for post-audit.

We recommended that Management:
Furnish the Audit team with any existing written policy on the granting of financial assistance that would systematically determine the extent of aid to be given to individuals in need.
Coordinate with the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Office to assess request for all assistance by way of Social Case Study Reports so that only the deserving indigents can be assisted and served. Beneficiaries be properly screened giving priority to the less privileged constituents.
Instruct the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Office to assist the applicants and require a complete submission of supporting documents for the assistance being requested as a basis of assessment and recommendation to make sure that only the deserving ones are provided with the necessary assistance.
In absence of any policy, create guidelines on the grant of donation/financial assistance containing the following:
Type of financial assistance (medical, burial etc.)
Approval limits for specific cases.
Criteria in granting the donation/financial assistance and the documentary requirements for each particular case.
Guidelines for the selection and retention of student-individual entitled for educational assistance.
Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure all disbursements for financial assistance be supported with appropriate and complete documentation pursuant to Section 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer, in coordination of the General Services Officer, submit to the Audit Team proofs of posting on the programs/projects undertaken in CY 2018, if any.  Likewise, ensure full compliance on the provisions of COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013 relative to the information and publicity of the Programs/Projects/Activities implemented by the municipality, their monthly updates and the display of signboards in conspicuous places within the location of the project regardless of nature, irrespective of amount.

We recommended that Management refrain the practice of paying for meals and snacks of officials and employees during ordinary staff meeting and Sangguniang Bayan (SB) sessions that are regular or normal activities in the LGU’s operations or part of the duties of the concerned officials/employees in order to prevent the incurrence of irregular and unnecessary expenditures and to ensure that government resources are used equitably and wisely.
Further, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant continue in checking the propriety and validity of the claims on meals and snacks unless these are duly supported with proper and complete documentation.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer observe the release of the barangay share from RPT collections on a quarterly basis, in order for the barangay to utilize the fund for its purpose.  Likewise, ensure that the payments are duly supported with complete documentation.

We recommended that Management exercise prudence and economy in the utilization of government funds by limiting the practice of undertaking travel as such that could have been used to finance more viable projects that would contribute to the improvement of general public. Likewise, require the concerned officials to render justification on the incurrence of said expenses. Moreover, adhere to the rules and regulation cited above to adopt austerity measures in Government.

We recommended that Management ensure that copies of contracts be furnished to the Auditor within five (5) working days from its execution and shall be supported with complete documents as required in COA Circular 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.  Likewise, the Municipal Engineer monitor and effect the inclusion of all awarded projects in his quarterly report of government projects.

Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Engineer coordinate with the Municipal Accountant and the Bids Chairman to furnish the Audit Team with the lacking documents specified in the Annex B and regularly observe completeness of contract documents submitted.

We recommended that Management require immediate settlement of the audit disallowances having been issued with COE to avoid further legal actions as prescribed by the aforementioned governing rules and regulations of the Circular. Likewise, strict compliance with laws, rules and regulations on the disbursements of funds to avoid issuances of audit suspensions, disallowances and charges in the future.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management do the following:

For the Road Networks not supported with proper documents

require the Inventory Committee to conduct an inventory of local roads under their jurisdiction and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of the Local Road Network at the end of every accounting period.

pursue complete reconciliation of the RPCLRN and the updated Registry of Public Infrastructures in coordination with the General Services Officer and the Accountant through detailed review of prior year’s transactions in order to identify any unrecorded road networks.

direct the Municipal Accountant to: 

maintain Local Road Network Ledger Card and maintain separate subsidiary ledgers for the road and its components namely: road lot, road pavement, drainage and slope protection structures, and other miscellaneous structures; 

support the beginning balance of Road Networks and its components amounting to P5,035,000.02  in the GL and Ledger Cards with the JEV and working papers; 

(c) make details of the recorded road, highways and bridges which were closed to government equity in the prior years and subsequently support the recording in its books of accounts the amount of road works equivalent to the required transition provision for CY 2017; and

(d) make disclose in the Notes to the Financial Statements as illustrated in Annex B of said circular. 

instruct the General Services Officer to (a) complete Local Road Inventory records with Road Map and Local Road Network Property Cards; and (b) submit the  Report on Local Road Network of the LGU prepared by the Inventory Committee as shown in Annex A of the circular.


For the unreconciled PPE

For accounts other than the Road Networks, require the GSO and the Municipal Accountant to revisit the items that caused the differences of physical count as against the Accounting books.

Ensure the compliance of the concerned officials and monitor their accomplishments.

supporting documents  before  granting  cash  advances  for  travel.
Likewise, advise the concerned 3 claimants to comment on  the  above
audit observation or refund the disallowed amount without  prejudice
to the issuance of Notice of Disallowance.

We recommended that Management  refrain  from  reimbursing  fuel
expenses of privately-owned vehicles used during  travels,  instead,
the officials and employees concerned shall reflect  the  equivalent
cost of fare using customary mode of  transportation  in  compliance
with COA Circular No. 96-004 and COA  Decision  No.  2014-123  dated

We recommended that the accountable officials concerned be required to liquidate their outstanding cash advances to settle their respective accountabilities and to reflect the fair balances of the Government Equity and the related asset and expense accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant exert effort to determine the real age or, if not possible, the approximate age of the receivable account-Due from NGOs/POs amounting to ₱100,000.00. Should it be ten years already, the Municipal Mayor may request for authority to write-off the receivable account from the Commission on Audit following the specific guidelines and procedures as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2016-005, thus, fairly present the affected accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The Management requested for write-off for the said amount and was granted in the level of Supervising Auditor and the Audit Team Leader.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the inventory committee to complete the physical count of the properties of the LGU and to submit the revised inventory report to the Office of the Auditor for verification. Also, we recommended that the inventory committee reconcile its inventory report with the records of the Municipal Accounting Office.
observations.managementAction: The Inventory Committee conducted a physical count and submitted the RPCPPE on January 31, 2018
observations.reason:
Inventory report and records of the Municipal Accounting office were not reconciled as of year-end thus,  
Reiterated in the Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the LGU’s records with that of the Provincial Government of Isabela to establish the correct balances of the reciprocal accounts.
observations.reason: Reiterated in the Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant determine the details of the ₱21,722.85 and the negative balance of ₱9,726.00 as basis for the necessary reclassification or adjustments, if any, as well transfer the amount of ₱104,720.00 from the General Fund to the Trust Fund to fairly present the balance of the account Other Payables in the books of the LGU.
observations.managementAction: Reclassification of accounts was made on the negative balance and the amount of ₱104,720.00. The latter was transferred to Trust Fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management stop the practice of disbursements on a reimbursement basis particularly on the procurement of goods and services. Accordingly, we recommended that management ensure that all purchases of goods and services are paid directly to the suppliers to comply with Section 93 of PD 1445 and to ensure collection of taxes from the suppliers concerned.

We recommended that management ensure that all disbursements of the municipality are supported with complete documentation. It is emphasized that the completeness of documentation includes not only the submission of the documents but also the consistency and correctness of the details thereof. 
In this regard, the lacking documents for the purchases of sheep and goats be submitted to prove the validity, legality and propriety thereof, as follows:
the lacking documents as required under COA Circular No. 2012-001; and 
the list of the beneficiaries of the dispersal which will be the basis for the conduct of confirmation; 
an explanation of the difference between the name of the payee-supplier in the check, disbursement voucher and Sales Invoice; and in the Purchase Order and Acknowledgement Receipt including the corrective document, if any.
Should the required documents be not submitted or should the results of the confirmation be negative, the disbursements may be disallowed in audit.
observations.managementAction: The Management directed the Municipal Agriculturist to submit the required list of beneficiaries.
observations.reason: Reiterated in the Part II of this report

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that management ensure that payment of gasoline expenses of the municipality be supported with complete documentation as prescribed. It is emphasized that complete documentation does not only mean the submission of documents but also the completeness of the details thereof. 
Accordingly, the lacking documents be submitted to ascertain the validity and propriety of the payment of gasoline amounting to ₱129,479.58, otherwise, the disbursement may be suspended or disallowed in audit.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain the timely submission of accounts monthly to facilitate the audit of the transactions.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant and Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer to prepare and submit, respectively, the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of the LDRRMF within the period prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-002.
observations.managementAction: Submission was made within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Engineer’s Office post or display a tarpaulin or signboard for all its projects at the project location pursuant to COA Circular No. 2013-004, thus, promote transparency. Also, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive inform the SA and ATL concerned within 10 days after the appropriate signboard and/or public notices are already posted as basis for validation.
observations.managementAction: Signboards of projects were posted within the project site.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor utilize the funds received from Jollibee Foods Inc. amounting to ₱104,720.00 for the purpose it was intended. Thereafter, the amount should be liquidated, otherwise, it should be returned to the source entity to reduce/eliminate the liability of the LGU.
observations.managementAction: A written representation dated April 21, 2017 was made to Jollibee Foundation for their approval of the request pending utilization of the fund.
observations.reason: Reiterated in the Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Accountant and BAC to have the deficiencies/lacking documents submitted in support of the DV covering the payment of 40.77% progress accomplishment for the Construction of Lenzon Municipal Road in the amount of P907,500.00 in conformance with COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to provide all construction joints under Item 311-PCPP with asphalt sealant in conformance to the finding and recommendation of the COA Technical Inspectors.
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to conduct periodic reconciliation of the reciprocal account balances with the PGI.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant of LGU Gamu is trying his best to reconcile their records with that of the PGI.
observations.reason: The balances of Due from LGU and Due to LGU as of September 31, 2016 in the books of the Provincial Government of Isabela and LGU Gamu, Isabela, remained unreconciled, hence, reiterated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the concerned LGU personnel to formulate a Solid Waste Management Plan for the municipality in compliance to RA 9003. We likewise recommended the provision for sanitary landfill as prescribed by the said law to be utilized by the municipality as waste disposal site.
observations.managementAction: Management already formulated a Solid Waste Management Plan (SWMP).
observations.reason: The SWMP has not yet been approved. Also, the LGU has not yet established a sanitary landfill of its own due to financial constraints. Hence, reiterated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasurer to abide with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 with regard to the due liquidation of cash advances for Petty Cash Fund and Payroll Fund within the period prescribed thereon and to see to it that both funds are settled at the end of each year.
observations.reason: Cash advances for petty cash was already fully liquidated as of December 31, 2016. However, cash advances for payroll fund still had a balance of P45,599.35 at year end. Hence, reiterated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the concerned municipal officials to: a) cause the immediate liquidation of their cash advances in compliance with COA Circular No. 97-002; b) require that no additional cash advances be granted to any official or employee unless the previous cash advance given is first settled; c) to ensure that all cash advances shall be fully liquidated at the end of each year; and d) direct that elected officials be granted cash advance only for their official travelling expenses. We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive directs the Municipal Accountant to send demand letters to officials and employees with outstanding cash advances and enforce prompt settlement and liquidation within the period prescribed by existing regulation.
observations.managementAction: Liquidations for cash advances for confidential funds in prior years were submitted to COA-CO
observations.reason: There are still unliquidated cash advances as of December 31, 2016, hence, reiterated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the submission of justification as to why cash advances were granted for purposes other than that prescribed under COA Circular No. 97-002. We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive directs the personnel concerned to refund their cash advances which were granted for purposes other than that prescribed under the said regulation. We likewise recommended that cash advances be granted only for the legally specified purposes prescribed under existing rules and regulations.
observations.managementAction: Liquidated most of the cash advances
observations.reason: Cash advances for irregular purposes were still granted during the current year, hence, reiterated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the submission, thru the Accountant, of the documents required by Joint Circular No. 2015-01 in support of the DVs covering the grant of cash advances for Confidential/Intelligence Fund. We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive directs the adherence to the said joint circular providing the guidelines on the entitlement, release, use, reporting and audit of CF/IF.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the creation of an inventory committee which shall be tasked to conduct and submit the LGU’s Annual Inventory Report on PPE/Report on the Physical Count of PPE on or before the 31st day of January of each year in conformance with existing regulation. We likewise reiterated our recommendation for the Local Chief Executive to direct the Municipal GSO to coordinate and reconcile his PPE records with the accounting office.
observations.managementAction: Physical inventory was conducted and the RPCPPE was prepared by the Inventory Committee and was submitted to the Office of the Auditor.
